params.description: Vertical picture graph appears on left. Scale of 2. Each symbol = 2.
Vertical picture graph appears on right. Scale of 5. Each symbol = 5. Key highlighted.

beats.text: In Warmup, you saw something important. Same data, but the scale of 5 graph needed fewer symbols. That's because each symbol represents MORE.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph on left. Books Read This Week data. Scale of 2.
Vertical picture graph on right. Same data. Scale of 5. Key highlighted.

beats.text: Same data, fewer symbols. That's why scale of 5 is useful when numbers get bigger. What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?
What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?

validator.description: Student selected 5, correct

beats.text: Right. Each symbol represents 5 items. That's the scale.

validator.description: Student selected 2, confusing scale of 2 with scale of 5

params.description: Key on scale of 5 graph highlights.

beats.text: You're looking at the scale of 2 graph. Look at the key on the scale of 5 graph. What does it say each symbol equals?

validator.description: Student selected 10, possibly doubling the scale value

params.description: Key on scale of 5 graph highlights.

beats.text: The key tells you exactly what each symbol stands for. Read the key on the scale of 5 graph. What number do you see?

validator.description: Student selected 1, confusing scale with counting by 1s

params.description: Key on scale of 5 graph highlights.

beats.text: When the scale is 5, each symbol represents 5 items, not 1. Look at the key. It shows what each symbol equals.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Key highlights showing Each ⭐ = 5. One symbol pulses to demonstrate the connection.

beats.text: Let me show you. The key says Each symbol equals 5. That means every symbol you see on this graph represents 5 items. When the scale is 5, each symbol stands for 5. That's what makes it a scale of 5.

params.description: Data table appears. Favorite Playground Activities. Swings: 20, Slides: 15, Monkey Bars: 25, Sandbox: 10. Vertical orientation.

beats.text: Here is a table showing what playground activities students voted for as their favorites. Let's see how 20 items become symbols when the scale is 5. Watch. We'll group by 5s.

beats.text: 5, 10, 15, 20. That's 4 groups of 5.

params.description: 4 groups transform into 4 symbols, stacking upward on picture graph for Swings column.

beats.text: 20 items equals 4 symbols. Each symbol shows 5.

beats.text: It's easier to read this picture graph if you can count by 5s. That's exactly what we do with scale of 5.

validator.description: Student answered 20, correct

beats.text: Right. 20 items. 5, 10, 15, 20. Skip counting tells you the total in the picture graph, and that matches the value in the data table.

validator.description: Student selected 4, treating symbol count as the total

params.description: Swings column highlights.

beats.text: You counted the symbols. But each symbol stands for more than 1. Check the key. What does each symbol represent? Skip count by the value in the key.

validator.description: Student selected 9, likely miscounting or using wrong scale

params.description: Key highlights on picture graph.

validator.description: Student selected 15, stopped counting before the fourth symbol

beats.text: You're counting by 5s. But check how many symbols are in the column. Count all of them: 5, 10, 15. That’s 3 symbols. How many symbols does Swings have?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights each symbol in sequence while counting: 5, 10, 15, 20.

beats.text: Let’s think through this. Look at the key: each symbol equals 5. Now skip count by 5s along the Swings category: one symbol is 5, two symbols 10, three symbols 15, four symbols 20. The picture graph shows 20 because we skip count by the scale.

params.description: Vertical data table titled Favorite Playground Activities appears. Categories: Swings, Slides, Monkey Bars, Sandbox. Values: Swings 20, Slides 15, Monkey Bars 25, Sandbox 10.

With a scale of 5, that's 10 votes. The activity with the fewest votes is Sandbox because it has the shortest column.

beats.text: Here's the same Playground data from the picture graph as a bar graph. You've read bar graphs before.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Favorite Playground Activities. Swings 4 symbols, Slides 3, Monkey Bars 5, Sandbox 2. Key: Each star = 5.
Vertical bar graph showing same data. Swings 20, Slides 15, Monkey Bars 25, Sandbox 10. Axis 0–30 in 5s.
Data table showing Playground Activities values.

beats.text: In a picture graph, you count by 5s using symbols: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25.

In a bar graph, you count by 5s using the numbers written along the side of the graph on the {axis}. Look: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. Same skip-counting, just written on the side. Both graphs count by 5s. Where do you see the 5s on the bar graph?
Where do you see the 5s on the bar graph?

validator.description: Student selected On the axis, correct

beats.text: Right. The axis shows the skip-counting: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. That's your scale of 5, built right into the side of the graph.

validator.description: Student selected 'In the symbols', confusing bar graph with picture graph format

params.description: Axis numbers highlight to draw attention to where the scale is shown.

beats.text: This is a bar graph, not a picture graph. Bar graphs don't use symbols. Look for the numbers on the bar graph..

validator.description: Student selected 'In the title', misunderstanding where scale information appears

params.description: Axis numbers highlight to draw attention to the scale.

beats.text: The title names what the graph is about. The scale is shown by numbers on the bar graph. Where are those numbers?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Animation traces up the axis, highlighting each number: 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30.

beats.text: Let’s find them together. Watch the axis. The numbers go 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. That's counting by 5s. The axis is where the scale lives in a bar graph. The axis shows scale of 5 on the bar graph.

params.description: Vertical picture graph titled Favorite Playground Activities. Categories: Swings (4 symbols), Slides (3 symbols), Monkey Bars (5 symbols), Sandbox (2 symbols). Scale 5: each symbol = 5. Key visible.
Vertical bar graph titled Favorite Playground Activities. Categories: Swings (20), Slides (15), Monkey Bars (25), Sandbox (10). Axis: 0 to 30 in intervals of 5. Side-by-side with picture graph.
Slides category highlights on picture graph.
Slides category highlights on bar graph.

What's the {height} of the Slides bar?

validator.description: Student selected 15, correct bar height

Axis numbers highlight to draw attention to the scale.

beats.text: Here’s new data to make a {scaled bar graph}. Students voted for their favorite lunch foods. Before you create the bars, look at the numbers on the {axis}.
These numbers count by 5s, just like you did with symbols. The axis does the skip-counting for you. What comes after 20 on this axis?
What comes after 20 on this axis?

validator.description: Student selected 25, correct

beats.text: Right. 25. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30... Same skip-counting you used for symbols, just written on the side.

validator.description: Student selected 21: counting by 1s instead of recognizing the scale of 5

params.description: Numbers 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 pulse on the axis to show the pattern.

beats.text: Look at the pattern on the axis. These numbers skip by 5s, not 1s. Each mark on the axis increases by 5. So what comes after 20?

validator.description: Student selected 30: skipped ahead by 10 instead of 5

params.description: Numbers 15, 20, 25 pulse to show the spacing between marks.

beats.text: Each step on this axis goes up by 5, not 10. From 15 to 20 is 5. From 20 to the next mark is another 5. Where will that mark be?

validator.description: Student selected 22: added 2 instead of following the scale pattern

params.description: All axis numbers from 0 to 20 pulse to show the consistent interval.

beats.text: This axis counts by 5s, just like you counted symbols. 5, 10, 15, 20, and then what? Keep counting by 5s.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Axis numbers highlight in sequence: 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, showing the counting by 5s pattern.

beats.text: Let me show you. The axis counts by 5s the whole way up: 0, 5, 10, 15, 20. After 20 comes 25. That’s 5 more than 20. That's how the scale works. Just like the symbols on a picture graph, but the skip-counting is built into the side of the bar graph.

beats.text: Let’s go through this step-by-step. Looking at the data table, Salad has 15 votes. I find 15 on the axis, 5, 10, 15, and set the bar to end there. Pasta has 25 votes. I find 25 on the axis, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and set that bar to end there. The bar height matches the value on the axis. That is how bar graphs work: the height shows the count.

params.title: Favorite Lunch Foods
params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Favorite Lunch Foods. Tacos=30, Pizza=25, Salad=15, Pasta=20, Sandwiches=15. Tacos and Salad bars highlighted.

beats.text: You already know how to compare using bar graphs when the scale is 1. Same skill works here when the axis counts by 5s. How many more students chose Tacos than Salad?
How many more students chose Tacos than Salad?

validator.description: Student selected 15, correct difference

beats.text: That's right. We need to {compare}. Tacos has 30 votes, Salad has 15. The difference is 15.

validator.description: Student selected 10, likely subtracted 5 instead of finding difference between 30 and 15

params.description: Tacos and Salad bars pulse to draw attention.

beats.text: Look carefully at where each bar ends on the axis. Then compare the values. What's the difference between the votes for Tacos and Salad?

validator.description: Student selected 30, reading Tacos value instead of finding the difference

params.description: Tacos and Salad bars pulse.

beats.text: The question asks how many more votes Tacos got than Salad. That means we compare. Tacos got 30 votes. How many votes for Salad? Then find the difference.

validator.description: Student selected 45, added the two values instead of finding the difference

params.description: Tacos and Salad bars pulse.

beats.text: When we ask how many more, we need to compare, not add. Subtract to find the difference. What's the difference between Tacos and Salad?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Visual bracket appears connecting Tacos bar at 30 and Salad bar at 15, showing the gap of 15.

beats.text: Let’s try together. Tacos ends at 30 on the axis. Salad ends at 15. To find how many more, we compare by subtracting: 30 minus 15 equals 15. The difference between the two bars is 15.

params.description: Data table appears showing numeric values only.

beats.text: So far, some parts of the graphs were already filled in for you. Now let's see if you can build a whole graph on your own.

params.description: Vertical data table appears. Books Read This Month. Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.
Horizontal picture graph appears. Books Read This Month. 0 categories filled. Key visible: Each ⭐ = 5 books.

beats.text: Four students tracked how many books they read this month and here is their data. This graph goes sideways, {horizontal}. Same idea as you build the graph, you just click for the {length} for the number of symbols instead of height.

Horizontal picture graph in building mode. Books Read This Month. Empty rows for Liam, Emma, Noah, Olivia. Key visible: Each star = 5 books.

Set the number of symbols for Liam.

beats.text: Not quite. Look at Liam's value: 35 books. The key shows each symbol equals 5 books. Count by 5s until you reach 35. How many 5s is that?

validator.description: Student placed wrong number of symbols three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: 7 symbols appear one by one in Liam row, skip-counting by 5s as each appears: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35.

beats.text: Let me show you. The table tells us Liam read 35 books. Each symbol stands for 5 books. I count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35. That's 7 times I counted 5. So I place 7 symbols. The answer is 7 symbols because 35 equals seven groups of 5.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears. Books Read This Month data. Liam row filled with 7 symbols. Emma, Noah, Olivia rows empty. Key: each symbol = 5 books.
Data table appears alongside graph showing Books Read This Month values: Liam 35, Emma 20, Noah 25, Olivia 40.

beats.text: Finish the picture graph. Use the data table and complete the graph.
Set the lengths for all remaining categories.

validator.description: Student placed Emma 4, Noah 5, Olivia 8, all correct

beats.text: Nice. You built the whole graph. Each symbol shows 5, and you used skip-counting to figure out how many symbols for each student.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect number of symbols on first attempt

beats.text: Not exactly. Check the data table and key. Count by 5s for each category as you place the symbols.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect number of symbols on second attempt

params.description: Data table rows highlight for Emma, Noah, and Olivia. Key highlights as well.

beats.text: Not quite. Emma read 20 books. Count by 5s until you reach 20. How many 5s is that? Place that number of symbols for Emma. Do the same process for Noah and Olivia.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect number of symbols three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Emma row fills with 4 symbols, Noah row with 5 symbols, Olivia row with 8 symbols.

beats.text: Let's try together. The key shows each symbol is 5 books read. Emma read 20 books. Count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20. Four groups of 5. That's 4 symbols. Noah read 25 books: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25. That's 5 symbols. Olivia read 40 books: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40. That's 8 symbols. Each category gets exactly the number of symbols that matches its value when you count by the scale.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears. Books Read This Month data. Liam=7 symbols (35 books), Emma=4 symbols (20 books), Noah=5 symbols (25 books), Olivia=8 symbols (40 books). Key visible: each symbol = 5 books.

beats.text: Now use your graph to find how many fewer books Emma read than Olivia.
How many fewer books did Emma read compared to Olivia?

validator.description: Student selected 20, correct difference between Olivia and Emma

beats.text: Right. Olivia read 40. Emma read 20. The difference is 20. Emma read 20 fewer books than Oliva this month.

validator.description: Student selected 15: possibly subtracted symbol count (8-4=4) then multiplied by 5 incorrectly, or confused which values to compare

params.description: Emma and Olivia rows highlight.

beats.text: Look at Emma and Olivia. Each symbol is 5 books read. Skip-count by 5s along the symbols in each row to find how many books each person read. What's the difference?

validator.description: Student selected 40: read Olivia's total instead of finding the difference

params.description: Emma and Olivia rows highlight.

beats.text: The question asks you to compare the number of books read by Emma to Olivia. You need the difference between them. Find how many each read, then subtract.

validator.description: Student selected 60: added Olivia and Emma's totals instead of finding the difference

params.description: Emma and Olivia rows highlight.

beats.text: How many fewer tells us to compare, not add. Find how many books Olivia read, then how many Emma read. Subtract to find the difference.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights Emma's row showing 4 symbols, counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20. Then highlights Olivia's row showing 8 symbols, counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40. Visual shows subtraction: 40 minus 20 equals 20.

beats.text: Here is how we work through this problem. We need to compare the books read by Emma and Olivia. Each symbol is 5 books read. Emma has 4 symbols. Count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20. That's 20 books. Olivia has 8 symbols: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40. That's 40 books. To compare we find the difference: 40 minus 20 is 20. Emma read 20 fewer books than Olivia this month.

params.description: Data table appears showing Books Read This Month: Liam 35, Emma 20, Noah 25, Olivia 40.

Horizontal picture graph appears titled Books Read This Month. Scale 5: each symbol = 5 books. Four rows show: Liam 7 symbols (35 books), Emma 4 symbols (20 books), Noah 5 symbols (25 books), Olivia 8 symbols (40 books). Key visible. Scale toggle control displays three options: Each symbol = 1, Each symbol = 2, Each symbol = 5. Defaults to scale of 5.

beats.text: You built this graph with scale of 5. But what would it look like with a different scale? Try it. Toggle the scale to 1. What happens?
Toggle the scale to 1. What happens?

validator.description: Student toggled scale from 5 to 1. Symbols multiply: Olivia 8 to 40, Liam 7 to 35, Noah 5 to 25, Emma 4 to 20. Key updates to Each symbol = 1.

params.description: Graph animates: symbols multiply. Olivia goes from 8 symbols to 40, Liam from 7 to 35, Noah from 5 to 25, Emma from 4 to 20. Key updates to Each symbol = 1.

beats.text: When you toggle to scale of 1, each symbol represents just 1 book. So Olivia's 40 books need 40 symbols. That's why the graph gets so long.

validator.description: Student selected scale of 2 instead of scale of 1

beats.text: You chose scale of 2. But the question asks you to toggle to 1. Look at the three options and click on scale of 1.

validator.description: Student selected scale of 5 instead of scale of 1

beats.text: The graph is already at scale of 5. The question asks you to change it to 1. Click on scale of 1.

validator.description: Student did not select scale of 1 after multiple attempts

params.description: Graph animates to scale of 1. Symbols multiply: Olivia from 8 to 40, Liam from 7 to 35, Noah from 5 to 25, Emma from 4 to 20. Key updates to Each symbol = 1.

beats.text: When you toggle to scale of 1, each symbol represents just 1 book. So Olivia's 40 books need 40 symbols. That's why the graph gets so long.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month: Liam 35, Emma 20, Noah 25, Olivia 40.
Horizontal picture graph, scale 5 (default). Liam 7 symbols, Emma 4 symbols, Noah 5 symbols, Olivia 8 symbols. Key: Each star = 5. Scale toggle active with three options.

beats.text: Olivia went from 8 symbols to 40 with scale of 1. That's a lot of symbols. Now try scale of 2.
Toggle the scale to 2.

validator.description: Student toggled scale from 1 to 2. Symbols reduce: Olivia 40 to 20, Liam 35 to 17.5, Noah 25 to 12.5, Emma 20 to 10. Key updates to Each symbol = 2.

params.description: Graph animates: symbols reduce. Olivia goes from 40 symbols to 20, Liam from 35 to 17.5 (half-symbol appears), Noah from 25 to 12.5 (half-symbol appears), Emma from 20 to 10. Key updates to Each symbol = 2.

beats.text: At scale of 2, each symbol represents 2 books. So Olivia's 40 books need 20 symbols. That's half as many as scale of 1, but still more than scale of 5.

validator.description: Student selected scale of 1 instead of scale of 2

beats.text: That's scale of 1, where you just were. The question asks you to toggle to 2. Click on scale of 2.

validator.description: Student selected scale of 5 instead of scale of 2

beats.text: That's scale of 5. We'll go there soon. First, try scale of 2. Click on scale of 2.

validator.description: Student did not select scale of 2 after multiple attempts

params.description: Graph animates to scale of 2. Symbols reduce: Olivia from 40 to 20, Liam from 35 to 17.5 with half-symbol, Noah from 25 to 12.5 with half-symbol, Emma from 20 to 10. Key updates to Each symbol = 2.

beats.text: At scale of 2, each symbol represents 2 books. So Olivia's 40 books need 20 symbols. That's half as many as scale of 1, but still more than scale of 5.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month: Liam 35, Emma 20, Noah 25, Olivia 40.
Horizontal picture graph, scale 1. Liam 35 symbols, Emma 20 symbols, Noah 25 symbols, Olivia 40 symbols. Key: Each star = 1. Scale toggle active.

beats.text: Scale of 2 has fewer symbols than scale of 1, but still more than scale of 5. Toggle back to 5 to compare.
Toggle the scale to 5.

beats.text: At scale of 5, each symbol represents 5 books. Olivia's 40 books need just 8 symbols. That's the original graph you built, the shortest version.

params.description: Graph animates to scale of 5. Symbols reduce: Olivia from 20 to 8, Liam from 17.5 to 7, Noah from 12.5 to 5, Emma from 10 to 4. Key updates to Each symbol = 5.

beats.text: At scale of 5, each symbol represents 5 books. Olivia's 40 books need just 8 symbols. That's the original graph you built, the shortest version.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month: Liam 35, Emma 20, Noah 25, Olivia 40.
Horizontal picture graph, scale 2. Liam 17.5 symbols, Emma 10 symbols, Noah 12.5 symbols, Olivia 20 symbols. Key: Each star = 2. Scale toggle active.

beats.text: Same 40 books for Olivia every time. Scale of 1: 40 symbols. Scale of 2: 20 symbols. Scale of 5: just 8. So which scale uses the fewest symbols to show the same information?
Which scale uses the fewest symbols?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 5, correct answer.

beats.text: Right. Scale of 5. Bigger scale, fewer symbols, same data.

params.description: Scale toggle becomes inactive after correct answer.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 1, which uses the most symbols

params.description: Olivia row highlights.

beats.text: Look at Olivia's row at each scale. At scale of 1, she had 40 symbols. At scale of 5, she had only 8. Which is scale uses the fewest symbols?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 2, which uses more symbols than scale of 5

params.description: Olivia row highlights.

beats.text: At scale of 2, Olivia had 20 symbols. At scale of 5, she had 8 symbols. Which scale shows a smaller number of symbols?

validator.description: Student did not select Scale of 5 after multiple attempts

params.description: Visual shows side-by-side comparison: Scale 1 with 40 symbols for Olivia, Scale 2 with 20 symbols, Scale 5 with 8 symbols.

beats.text: Scale of 5 uses the fewest symbols. When each symbol represents MORE books, you need FEWER symbols. That's why bigger scales are useful when numbers get large.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month: Liam 35, Emma 20, Noah 25, Olivia 40.
Horizontal picture graph, scale 5. Liam 7 symbols, Emma 4 symbols, Noah 5 symbols, Olivia 8 symbols. Key: Each star = 5. Scale toggle active with three options.

beats.text: All three picture graphs showed the same information. The scale just changes how many symbols you need.

params.description: Horizontal Data table appears. This Week's Screen Time (minutes). Saturday=35, Sunday=30, Monday=40, Tuesday=25. 4 categories displayed numerically.
Horizontal bar graph appears. Bars extend sideways from left. Axis along bottom labeled 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45. All 4 bars empty.

beats.text: Here is new data showing the minutes of screen time used this week. This bar graph is {horizontal}. The bars go sideways. But look at the axis along the bottom. Same skip-counting by 5s. What comes after 35 on this axis?
What comes after 35 on this axis?

validator.description: Student selected 40, correct

beats.text: Right. 40. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15... and keep going 35, 40, 45. Same pattern, just running sideways.

validator.description: Student selected 36 — counting by 1s instead of recognizing the scale of 5

params.description: Axis numbers highlight to draw attention to the scale pattern.

beats.text: Look at the numbers on the axis. They don't go 31, 32, 33. They jump by 5s. So what comes after  35?

validator.description: Student selected 45 — skipped ahead, missing the next value in the sequence

params.description: Axis numbers highlight to draw attention to the sequence.

beats.text: 45 is on the axis, but it's not right after 35. Count from 30 by 5s. What do you get?

validator.description: Student selected 30 — went backward instead of forward in the sequence

params.description: Axis numbers highlight to draw attention to the direction.

beats.text: 30 comes before 35, not after. The axis counts up: 0, 5, 10, 15... What's the next jump after 35?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Axis numbers highlight in sequence: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40.

beats.text: Here's how the axis works. It counts by 5s: 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35. After 35, add 5 more to follow the pattern. That gives us 40. That's how a scale of 5 works on a bar graph axis.

params.description: Data table appears. This Week's Screen Time (minutes). Saturday=35, Sunday=30, Monday=40, Tuesday=25.
Empty horizontal bar graph template appears. Weekend Screen Time. Scale marked 0, 5, 10... up to 45.

beats.text: Now let's build the horizontal bar graph. This data shows how many minutes of screen time one person used over 4 days. Read the data, set the lengths of the bars to complete the graph.
Create the bar graph. Set all four bars.

validator.description: All four bars correctly set to match data table values

beats.text: That's right. You created the bar graph independently. Horizontal or vertical, you've got this.

validator.description: Student set one or more bars incorrectly on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Look at the data table and the axis. Match each value to where it lands on the scale.

validator.description: Student set one or more bars incorrectly on second attempt

params.description: Axis numbers highlight to show the skip-counting pattern.

beats.text: Let's think about this. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45. For each day, find the number in the data table, then set the bar to end at that number on the axis. Where does Monday's 40 minutes stop? Do this for all 4 days.

validator.description: Student set one or more bars incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates setting all four bars. Saturday bar extends to 35, Sunday bar extends to 30, Monday bar extends to 40, Tuesday bar extends to 25.

beats.text: Here's how we can approach this. Saturday has 35 minutes, so I set the Saturday bar to end at 35 on the axis. Sunday has 30, so the bar ends at 30. Monday has 40, the bar ends at 40. Tuesday has 25, the bar ends at 25. The bar length shows the value. Each bar ends right on the axis number that matches the data table.

params.title: Weekend Screen Time
params.description: Horizontal bar graph appears. This Week's Screen Time data. Saturday=35, Sunday=30, Monday=40, Tuesday=25.
Saturday and Sunday bars highlight.

beats.text: Let's use your bar graph to answer a question. How many minutes of screen time on Saturday and Sunday in all?
How many minutes of screen time on Saturday and Sunday in all?

validator.description: Student selected 65, correct answer (35 + 30)

beats.text: Right. 65 minutes in all. We need to combine. Saturday has 35, Sunday has 30. 35 plus 30 is 65. That's how you add values from a bar graph.

validator.description: Student selected 5, the scale increment instead of the sum or subtracted

params.description: Saturday and Sunday bars highlight.

beats.text: To find the minutes from Saturday and Sunday in all, we need to combine.. We need the total for these two days. Find the length of the Saturday and Sunday bars and add their values

validator.description: Student selected 35, only Saturday's value

params.description: Saturday and Sunday bars pulse to draw attention to both.

beats.text: There were 35 minutes of screen time on Saturday. The question asks for Saturday AND Sunday. Find Sunday's value, then add them together.

validator.description: Student selected 70, possibly doubled 35 or added Monday's value

params.description: Saturday and Sunday bars highlight separately, one after the other.

beats.text: Let's check each bar carefully. We need the minutes from Saturday and Sunday. How many minutes are shown by these bars?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System traces Saturday bar from 0 to 35, then Sunday bar from 0 to 30, showing the values on axis.

beats.text: Let's look at how this is done together. Saturday's bar ends at 35. Sunday's bar ends at 30. How many minutes in all means we combine them. 35 plus 30 equals 65. The answer is 65 minutes in all. We read each bar's height, then add those values together.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph titled 'Books Read This Month' appears. Four categories: Liam 7 symbols, Emma 4 symbols, Noah 5 symbols, Olivia 8 symbols. Key visible: each symbol = 5 books.
Horizontal bar graph titled 'Weekend Screen Time (minutes)' appears. Four category rows: Saturday 35, Sunday 30, Monday 40, Tuesday 25. Horizontal axis 0–45 in intervals of 5.

beats.text: You made both kinds of graphs today. Here's one last question. Picture graphs use symbols. Bar graphs use bars. What do these graphs have in common?
What do these graphs have in common?
options: They both use the same scale

condition.selected: They both use the same scale

validator.description: Student selected 'They both use the same scale', correct answer

beats.text: Right. Both use scale of 5. Picture graphs show it with symbols. Count by 5s. Bar graphs show it on the side. Same scale, different format.

validator.description: Student selected 'They both use symbols'—missed that bar graphs don't use symbols

params.description: Saturday bar highlights on bar graph.

beats.text: No, that’s not it. Look at the bar graph. Do you see symbols? Bar graphs use bars, not symbols. What do the two graphs have in common?

validator.description: Student selected 'They look the same'—didn't notice the visual difference between symbols and bars

params.description: Liam row highlights on picture graph, showing symbols.
Saturday bar highlights on bar graph.

beats.text: They don’t look exactly the same. One uses symbols. One uses bars. They show data in different formats. Look for something that is the same.

validator.description: Student selected 'They both have 5 categories'—counted categories instead of focusing on scale

params.description: Categories are highlighted in both the graphs to draw student’s attention to the category count on both.

beats.text: Count the categories. Picture graph has 4: Liam, Emma, Noah, Olivia. Bar graph has 4: Saturday, Sunday, Monday, Tuesday. They both have 4 categories, but not 5. What else do you notice?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Picture graph key highlights. Symbols pulse in sequence: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40.
Bar graph axis numbers highlight in sequence: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45.

beats.text: Watch. Picture graph: each symbol equals 5. Count by 5s. Bar graph: the axis shows 5, 10, 15, 20, and so on. Count by 5s. Both graphs use scale of 5. That's what they have in common. Same way of counting, different format for showing it. Bar graphs on the axis. Picture graphs along the symbols.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Scale of 5. Shows completed work from prior section.

beats.text: You made picture graphs and bar graphs, both with scale of 5. You used skip-counting to figure out symbols and bar heights or lengths. Let's see what you know.
